frameworks.name: redundant-target effect / race-model inequality
frameworks.one_line: RT speed-up for double targets tested against bound predicted by independent parallel channels

frameworks.name: independent-processing model of identity and expression
frameworks.one_line: Bruce-Young style separability of facial identity and emotion channels

core_question: Does aging change how facial identity and emotional expression are integrated during face processing?
key_assumptions: identity and expression can in principle be processed by independent parallel channels
violation of race-model inequality indexes co-active / interactive processing
redundancy-gain magnitude reflects strength of cross-dimensional integration
manipulations: target composition (identity-only, expression-only, redundant identity+expression, neither)
key_findings: all age groups faster on redundant identity+expression targets than on single-target faces
redundancy gains exceeded the bound predicted by independent processing of identity and emotion
older adults showed reliably larger redundancy gains than the young group
the integration pattern held across the full adult age range
interpretations: facial identity and expression are processed co-actively rather than independently
greater lifetime experience with faces enhances cross-dimensional integration in older adults
limitations_noted: alternative explanations for the age-related gain enlargement are acknowledged
